proposed_revisions.rationale: All 9 partial_only solutions cover exclusively asset.compliance through inspection and safety management capabilities, indicating compliance_inspection should be isolated as a standalone, minimal core. The remaining lifecycle, maintenance, and operational capabilities naturally cluster together as a single comprehensive asset operations core, reflecting how vendors typically separate compliance tooling from integrated asset management platforms.

backing_evidence.reason: Ailytics covers only asset.compliance; all other caps remain uncovered, validating compliance as a discrete, separable concern.
SafetyCulture explicitly addresses asset.compliance and inspection audit trail but has zero lifecycle_maintenance coverage, confirming the two domains are orthogonal.
Novade solutions cover 1 of 9 capability IDs (asset.compliance), demonstrating that compliance is a thin, isolated slice that should not anchor a multi-cap core.

proposed_revisions.rationale: A three-core split preserves the existing EAM/CMMS distinction while isolating compliance as a standalone, optional module. The partial_only patterns show that compliance vendors are orthogonal to both EAM and CMMS; separating it removes a structural mismatch and allows clearer, thinner core definitions that align with how the market actually segments asset management solutions.

backing_evidence.reason: CSG Novade Safety-HSE covers inspection and compliance only, with zero lifecycle_maintenance; compliance as a separate core removes this cross-core gap.
Hubble Safety Management System demonstrates that compliance/safety is a standalone domain with no overlap to EAM register or CMMS maintenance operations.
SafetyCulture ePTW covers audit trail and compliance caps but fails to address any lifecycle or maintenance capability, validating compliance as an independent, optional layer.

proposed_revisions.rationale: The partial_only solutions consistently cover the operational capabilities (item master, stock levels, reorder rules, purchase orders, vendor master) together but fail on analytics and barcode scanning. This suggests vendors naturally bundle transactional inventory and procurement operations as one integrated core, while treating analytics and scanning as a separate, optional enhancement. The current split artificially separates procurement from its natural inventory context.

backing_evidence.reason: Covers all 5 operational caps (item_master, stock_levels, purchase_orders, vendor_master) but fails only on barcode_scanning and supply_chain_analytics.
Covers all 5 operational caps (item_master, stock_levels, reorder_rules, purchase_orders, vendor_master) but missing only barcode_scanning and supply_chain_analytics.
Demonstrates the same pattern: all 5 operational caps covered, failures isolated to barcode_scanning and supply_chain_analytics.
Covers 3 of 5 operational caps (item_master, stock_levels, reorder_rules) with no procurement module coverage, illustrating that procurement_analytics fails as a standalone core.

proposed_revisions.rationale: An alternative framing that recognizes barcode scanning as a core inventory execution capability (alongside item master and stock levels) and consolidates reorder rules with procurement logic, treating analytics as a planning/decision-support layer. This split reflects how solutions separate basic inventory tracking from procurement workflows and planning analytics.

backing_evidence.reason: Covers item_master and stock_levels (core inventory) plus purchase_orders and vendor_master (procurement/planning), failing only on barcode_scanning and reorder_rules integration.
Explicitly covers barcode/QR scanning (core inventory execution) but lacks procurement and planning, validating barcode_scanning as a distinct inventory operations requirement.
Covers item_master and stock_levels in one group, purchase_orders and vendor_master in the other, with reorder_rules as a bridge to planning logic.

proposed_revisions.rationale: Vendors consistently deliver payroll and statutory reporting together as an integrated compliance package, while core HR operations (employee master, leave, attendance, self-service) form a distinct operational backbone. Talent management (recruitment, onboarding, performance) clusters separately because many vendors specialize in either operational HR or talent/learning, rarely both. This split reflects actual market segmentation.

backing_evidence.reason: BAS covers all three proposed cores partially, validating the grouping of payroll + statutory + benefits, HR ops, and performance as natural vendor packages.
BIPO's strength in payroll_processing, payslips, statutory_reporting, and compensation_benefits confirms payroll_statutory as a cohesive core.
HReasily's partial coverage across payroll (processing, slips, statutory, compensation) and HR ops (master, leave, attendance) validates the payroll_statutory and core_hr_operations split.
AIO's exclusive focus on recruitment_ats and onboarding_offboarding demonstrates talent_performance as a standalone vendorable core separate from payroll and operations.
JustLogin's coverage of time_attendance and leave_management only affirms core_hr_operations as a distinct, operationally-focused core.

proposed_revisions.rationale: Payroll processing is operationally bundled with core HR functions (leave, attendance, master data) in most vendor offerings, while statutory compliance and benefits administration form a specialized compliance-focused subset. This reflects the reality that many operational HR systems include basic payroll processing, but fewer achieve full statutory and benefits complexity. The talent core remains unchanged.

backing_evidence.reason: Carbonate HR groups employee_master, leave, attendance with payroll_processing as one operational unit, suggesting payroll_processing belongs in HR administration core.
BIPO's tight pairing of leave_management and self_service_portal with payroll_processing shows operational HR and basic payroll as a natural administrative core.
IMPACT BEST's focus on payslips, statutory_reporting, and compensation_benefits (without basic operational HR) suggests these form a distinct compliance layer.
HReasily's architecture splits operational capabilities (master, leave, attendance) + payroll from statutory and compensation, validating the two-payroll-core model.
